text: Hey everyone, welcome to Talks of Google. I'm Map on Jovi and I'm thrilled to be joined today by Oliver Raider.
Oliver is a journalist and author who has been a senior writer at 538 and editor of the Riddleur Puzzle Column, a collection of endlessly entertaining math puzzles.
Oliver recently studied artificial intelligence as a Neemin fellow at Harvard University and holds a PhD in economics with a focus on game theory.
Oliver is here with us today to discuss whose newest book, Seven Games, a Human History, which is a biography of Seven Enduring and Beloved Games, from chess to poker to go and a few others.

In his book Oliver examines the story of why and how we humans play these games, he delves into the delightful arcana of their rules and looks at the interplay between elite champions of these games and the emerging AI players that have come to dominate in several realms.
Now throughout our talk, you might have some great questions pop up in your head and when you do, please go ahead and add them to the chat on the right.
We will have time shortly for Oliver to answer some of these so be sure to get your questions in early.
But first, Oliver, thank you so much for being here.
My pleasure, thank you so much for having me.
So your book does a great job of both looking in depth at these seven games that you present while also being thoughtful on some of the big, almost philosophical questions around gaming.
One of those questions that I want to start with is why do we play games?
Yes, it's a great question. A natural one. Why did I write a whole book about these things?
Yeah, I think there's a constellation of answers.
I think the simplest but maybe at least satisfactory answer is that games are fun.
Games are ways to occupy our time in an enjoyable way.
But I think there's deeper, more interesting answers as well.
One of these is that games are practice.
There's a famous story that I was told many times when I was reporting this book.
Imagine that you're a prehistoric human and you have to hunt to gather food.
There's a few ways you could go about this. You could hunt all the time, but this is incredibly dangerous.
You could never hunt, but that's no good either because then you never get any good at hunting.
Or you could invent a game.
Through this rock at that tree, first person to hit it wins.
And the game is a way to sort of practice for the real world.
And indeed today, instead of rather less violent times, games are still practice.
They distill elements of the real world.
So a game like chess allows us to practice strategy and planning ahead.
A game like backgammon allows us to grapple with the uncertainty of the world,
to be the roles of a dice, a game like poker.
We're confronted with hidden information and deception and on and on.
So games are sort of a form of practice for the real world.
The third answer that I would throw into the mix is that games are in art form
and that playing games is a form of art appreciation.
I mean that not in a grand deos way, but in a literal way.
So whereas painting captures the visual world and music captures the heard world,
games capture agency, human agency, these sort of modes of deciding and deliberating and acting.
And we humans like art appreciation, I think games are one mode of that.
Yeah, that's interesting at that point about agency because it's also, it seems like you can
interact in ways that you wouldn't necessarily in everyday life.
You can create these scenarios where you can exercise a different kind of agency.
Yeah, that's right. I mean, it's not often that I get to lead an army
into battle in the real world, probably for the best, but when I play chess or Stratego or any sort of modern
game that takes place in that milieu, I can do. We get to occupy states of agency that
then are in other ways available to us. I think that's a great point. That's certainly another appeal.
Yeah, and are you practicing to lead an army somewhere? Is that something we should be concerned about?
I'm not at liberty to talk about that. That's good. But I will say that I think that I do think that the
the border between games and real life is a porous one. I think that we do legitimately learn things
from games and take them to our real lives. I can tell you, give you a personal example which is I used to
be deeply, deeply obsessed with Scrabble. The key dynamic in Scrabble is an inter-temperal one, a trade-off
between today and tomorrow, or in Scrabble this turn and the next turn. This is the sort of dynamic
programming problem. Scoring points today, leaving yourself good tiles for next turn. I think I
very legitimately think I learned lessons about saving and being kind to my future self through
my Scrabble obsession. I think this is a real thing, and I'm sure I'm not alone.
Yeah. Yeah, because also with the scope of games, there's that immediate payoff or damage where you're
like, oh, I should have saved that S until next turn. You don't have to wait the way that you do in
life sometimes to get hit in the face of your mistake. That's right. The lessons come fast and furious.
Like I can play a lot of games of Scrabble or a lot of Handsipoker or whatever it is.
This sort of is a miniature, crystallized little models of the world. Yeah, definitely.
Now from the very earliest days of computing, researchers and programmers have been tinkering with games.
Why even early on was there this natural marriage between computing and games?
Well, again, I think there's a few answers. I think one is sort of what we've
already been talking about. I think if games are practice for the real world for humans,
games are practice for the real world for computers. They're appealing to computer scientists for a number of
reasons. One is, you know, they're small and manageable. You can, again, just like a humans, you can
play a lot of them in a row. They're very easily measurable. So you can put a computer against
another computer and games have winners and losers. And that's that's data that you're generating.
Or you can put a computer versus a human. And if the computer wins well, hey, in this very limited domain,
I have very good reasons to suspect this computer is better than the human. And I think closely related to
that is games, chess seemingly chief among them, has long been sort of tied up with the concept of
intelligence itself. So if you look back at, you know, the document proposing this famous AI
symposium at Dartmouth and the 50s. And they said, we envision computers doing, you know, the highest
columns of human kind and they list of them. And they was like writing music and proving mathematical
theorems and playing chess. These were like the three things they wanted their AI to be able to do. So
I think it's practical. On the one hand, it's practical. Games are a very practical thing to unleash your
your system on. And two, it's sort of this romantic notion of this high, these higher ideals of
humanity. Yeah, yeah, that's an interesting point. And kind of going into some of the earlier AI's
around games, you talk in your book about checkers and some of the early projects around building AI

uh, a cook comes, uh, most likely comes from the phrase, um, your goose is cooked as in your,
your checkers opponents goose is cooked and checkers like, like, I think every game in my book and,

Over a 40 year stretch of play, comprising some 1000 tournament games, Tinsley lost exactly three

Yeah. Yeah. And I think it's what you mentioned there about Tinsley's, um, record is quite impressive,

Yeah. And as those early AIs became competitive, how did the players, uh, in checkers and then other games

That fairly, that fairly quickly dissolves, uh, once, once they start losing to the machine. And,

pretty rapid transition from, you know, what the heck is this machine doing here to, hey,
can I have that machine so, so that I can learn from it? Yeah. Yeah, it's quite extraordinary. I think
also the, the pace at which change can happen when, um, obviously when it's humans, you have, um,

So then when you have this extra piece where you can have, um, this additional knowledge comes

based increase in the level of play in internaments and at the competitive level. Yeah, yeah, and one of the

sort of middling novice journalist like me, novice chess. And I can sort of get a sense of what's going on

Right. Oh, I see the thermometer move up, you know, wow, what a terrible move by white or by black,
